g.data-narration: Welcome to Virtualization for Data Center Automation. This lecture explores how virtualization enables automated data center operations.
Data center automation uses software tools to manage operations with minimal human intervention, covering provisioning, monitoring, and recovery.
g.text: Automated management of IT resources with minimal human intervention.
Covers provisioning, configuration, monitoring, maintenance, and recovery

g.data-narration: Virtualization provides the abstraction layer that decouples software from hardware, enabling automation through logical resource management.
g.text: Abstracts physical hardware into logical, software-defined entities
Enables management through software interfaces instead of physical configurations

g.data-narration: The architecture stack shows how applications run on virtual machines, which operate on hypervisors, ultimately running on physical hardware.
Type 1 hypervisors run directly on hardware for production use, while Type 2 hypervisors run on OS for testing and development.
g.text: Examples: VMware ESXi, Hyper-V, Xen, KVM

g.data-narration: Containers virtualize the OS instead of entire machines, offering lighter weight virtualization with faster startup and higher density.
Five key features make virtualization powerful: resource pooling, abstraction, isolation, encapsulation, and hardware independence.
g.text: CPU, Memory, Storage, Network aggregated into shared pools → Allocated dynamically

g.data-narration: Automated provisioning uses VM templates and Infrastructure as Code to deploy resources consistently through code-defined infrastructure.
Live migration moves running VMs between physical hosts without downtime for maintenance, load balancing, and energy savings.
Dynamic resource scheduling automatically balances workloads across hosts based on CPU, memory, and storage utilization metrics.
Vertical scaling adds resources to existing VMs, while horizontal scaling adds more VM instances to handle increased load automatically.
Disaster recovery includes snapshot-based recovery, continuous VM replication, and automated failover for business continuity.
Key tools include VMware vSphere and OpenStack for orchestration, Kubernetes for containers, and Ansible, Terraform for automation.
Key benefits include increased efficiency through consolidation, improved agility for faster deployment, and significant cost reductions.
Implementation requires attention to performance overhead (1-15% CPU), memory management, security hardening, and managing VM sprawl.
Future trends include hyperconverged infrastructure, serverless computing, AI-driven automation, and edge computing integration.
